ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Basundi; mapped to the closest parent class 'milk-based dessert food product' (FOODON_03301116). A more specific code should be requested from the FoodOn maintainers.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Milk-based dessert) is the closest defensible match. No specific Basundi code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Basundi or a sufficiently close milk-based Indian dessert as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Basundi is approximately 150 g (one small bowl), which is the typical portion served at Indian festivals and family meals.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Basundi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for khoa/mawa (reduced milk) and sweetened condensed milk, adjusted for the typical sugar addition (~10–12 g/100 g) and nut garnish contribution. Macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Milk, Indian buffalo, fluid' (FDC ID 170877) and 'Sweetened condensed milk' (FDC ID 172217). Energy cross-checked via Atwater factors: protein 5.8 × 4 = 23.2 kcal, fat 8.5 × 9 = 76.5 kcal, carbohydrate 16.5 × 4 = 66.0 kcal; total = 165.7 kcal ≈ 165 kcal. Values are plausible and internally consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on sweetened condensed milk and khoa
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Basundi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. GI of 45 is estimated by analogy with sweetened condensed milk (GI ~61, University of Sydney) and khoa/reduced milk (GI ~35–40, estimated from whole-milk reduction studies). The high protein (5.8 g) and fat (8.5 g) content of Basundi slow gastric emptying and glucose absorption, supporting a GI in the low range (~40–50). A midpoint of 45 is used. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 24.75) / 100 = 11.1 g (using 150 g serving with 16.5 g carbohydrate/100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat and moderate sodium from full-cream milk; potassium and calcium content partially offset risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Basundi to occasional small servings (≤100 g) due to its saturated fat content (~5.2 g/100 g), while noting that its potassium (155 mg/100 g) and calcium (130 mg/100 g) content may provide modest blood-pressure benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content (14.5 g/100 g) with significant glycemic load per serving (~11 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Basundi because its high sugar content (14.5 g/100 g) and glycemic load of approximately 11 per 150 g serving will cause meaningful postprandial blood glucose elevation; sugar-free variants using low-GI sweeteners may be considered under dietitian guidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.2 g/100 g) and added sugar (~14.5 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic and inflammatory risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Basundi only occasionally and in small portions (≤100 g), as its saturated fat and high sugar content may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels; nut garnishes (almonds, pistachios) provide beneficial unsaturated fats but do not offset the overall saturated fat load.

lactose_intolerance.key_factor: Made entirely from full-cream milk; high lactose content
lactose_intolerance.guidance: Basundi is not suitable for individuals with lactose intolerance as it is prepared by concentrating full-cream milk, which increases the lactose concentration per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basundi is a traditional Indian milk-based dessert originating in the states of Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Karnataka. It is prepared by slow-cooking full-cream milk in a heavy-bottomed pan over low heat, continuously stirring and folding the cream layer (malai) back into the milk until the volume reduces to approximately half. The resulting product is thick, rich, and slightly grainy in texture — similar to a pourable rabri — with a deep, caramelised milk flavour. Once the desired consistency is achieved, sugar is added along with aromatic spices including green cardamom, saffron, and nutmeg. The dessert is chilled before serving and garnished generously with slivered almonds, pistachios, and cashews. Nutritionally, Basundi provides approximately 165 kcal per 100 g, with 5.8 g protein, 8.5 g fat (of which ~5.2 g is saturated), and 16.5 g carbohydrate (14.5 g sugar). It is a good source of calcium (130 mg/100 g), potassium (155 mg/100 g), riboflavin (0.25 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (0.6 mcg/100 g). Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), though the glycemic load per 150 g serving is ~11, making it a moderate glycemic concern for people with Type 2 Diabetes. Basundi is a celebratory dish served during festivals such as Gudi Padwa, Diwali, Janmashtami, and Raksha Bandhan, and is traditionally paired with hot, fluffy Puris. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal, but is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between Basundi and Rabri?
What festivals is Basundi served at?
Can I eat Basundi if I have high blood pressure?
What nuts are used in Basundi?

confidence_reason: Basundi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN published tables. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for reduced milk (khoa/mawa) and sweetened condensed milk, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Energy is internally consistent via Atwater factors. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using sweetened condensed milk and reduced-milk analogues. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: Basundi is made from reduced full-cream milk with sugar and spice flavourings; the nut garnish contributes negligible fibre at typical garnish quantities, and the base product contains naturally zero dietary fibre — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN reduced-milk data. 2. water_g is set to -1: detailed moisture data for home-prepared Basundi at the specific reduction ratio used in this recipe are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. Saturated fat (5.2 g), monounsaturated fat (2.1 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.3 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g), and cholesterol (28 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for whole milk (FDC ID 170860) scaled for concentration factor (~2×) and sugar addition; these are approximate values. 4. The food_group has been corrected from 'Snacks' (input) to 'Desserts' as Basundi is a milk-based sweet dessert, not a savoury snack. 5. Portions in the input contained four identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single 150 g serving. 6. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Basundi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 7. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for Basundi exists as of 2024.
